name: Countries of Europe
description: All sovereign nations of Europe -- identify countries by their capitals, geography, and cultural clues

questions.question: What European country has Tirana as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Tirana. Population ~2.8 million. Official language: Albanian, one of the oldest and most distinct branches of the Indo-European language family. Majority Muslim (~57%), with significant Orthodox Christian and Catholic minorities. Parliamentary republic. Albania declared independence from the Ottoman Empire in 1912. Under communist dictator Enver Hoxha (1944-1985), the country was one of the most isolated in the world, banning religion entirely and severing ties with both the USSR and China. Hoxha ordered the construction of over 170,000 concrete bunkers across the country to repel an invasion that never came. Notable figures: Mother Teresa (born to Albanian parents in Skopje), Ismail Kadare (novelist, perennial Nobel Prize contender), and Fan Noli (writer, politician, and bishop). Major industries: mining (chromium, copper), textiles, agriculture. Albania's coastline faces Italy across the Adriatic Sea, with the narrowest point only 45 miles wide at the Strait of Otranto. The country is roughly the size of Maryland. Albanians call their country Shqiperia, meaning 'Land of Eagles.' Albania was one of the last European countries to get television, with broadcasts beginning in 1971. The traditional Albanian code of honor, the Kanun, includes the practice of sworn virgins (women who take a vow of celibacy to live as men).

questions.question: What tiny European country is nestled in the Pyrenees mountains between France and Spain?
questions.explanation: Capital: Andorra la Vella, the highest capital city in Europe at 3,356 feet elevation. Population ~80,000. Official language: Catalan (the only sovereign state where Catalan is the sole official language). Predominantly Roman Catholic. Andorra is a parliamentary co-principality with two co-princes: the President of France and the Bishop of Urgell in Spain, a system dating back to a feudal charter in 1278. The country has no airport, no railway, and no military (France and Spain are responsible for its defense). Area: only 181 square miles, roughly 2.5 times the size of Washington, D.C. Andorra was technically at war with Germany from World War I until 1958 because it was inadvertently left out of the Treaty of Versailles. Major industries: tourism (skiing and duty-free shopping attract about 8 million visitors per year, making it the most visited country per capita), banking, and retail. Andorra has no income tax, no capital gains tax, and no inheritance tax. Life expectancy is among the highest in the world at over 83 years. The country did not have a formal constitution until 1993. Andorra joined the United Nations in 1993 and is not a member of the EU. Its national dish is escudella, a hearty stew.

questions.question: What European country has Vienna as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Vienna. Population ~9 million. Official language: German. Predominantly Roman Catholic (~55%). Federal parliamentary republic. Austria-Hungary was one of the great European powers until its dissolution after World War I. The modern republic was established in 1918. Austria was annexed by Nazi Germany (the Anschluss) in 1938 and regained sovereignty in 1955, pledging permanent neutrality. Notable figures: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart, Ludwig van Beethoven (born in Bonn but career in Vienna), Franz Schubert, Gustav Klimt, Sigmund Freud, Ludwig Wittgenstein, Erwin Schrodinger, Arnold Schwarzenegger, Adolf Hitler (born in Braunau am Inn). Major industries: machinery, vehicles, pharmaceuticals, tourism (especially skiing and cultural tourism). Austria is roughly the size of South Carolina. The Alps cover about 62% of the country. Vienna has been ranked the world's most livable city multiple times. The Habsburgs ruled from Vienna for over 600 years (1282-1918). Austria is landlocked, bordered by eight countries: Germany, Czech Republic, Slovakia, Hungary, Slovenia, Italy, Switzerland, and Liechtenstein. The Spanish Riding School in Vienna, founded in 1572, is the oldest riding school in the world. The first postcards were introduced in Austria in 1869.

questions.question: What Eastern European country lies between Poland and Russia, with Minsk as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Minsk. Population ~9.4 million. Official languages: Belarusian and Russian (Russian dominates daily use). Predominantly Eastern Orthodox Christian (~48%), with a Catholic minority. Presidential republic in form, but widely considered Europe's last dictatorship under Alexander Lukashenko, who has ruled since 1994. Belarus declared independence from the Soviet Union in 1991. The name Belarus means 'White Rus,' referring to the medieval Slavic region. About 40% of the country is forested, including parts of the ancient Bialowieza Forest (shared with Poland), one of the last primeval forests in Europe and home to the European bison. Notable figures: Marc Chagall (born in Vitebsk), Svetlana Alexievich (2015 Nobel Prize in Literature), software developers behind World of Tanks (Wargaming). Major industries: heavy machinery (tractors, trucks), potash (one of the world's largest producers), refined petroleum products, IT services. Belarus is flat, with its highest point only 1,132 feet above sea level. The country lost roughly a quarter of its population in World War II, the highest proportion of any country. Minsk was almost completely destroyed in WWII and rebuilt in Soviet monumental style. Belarus still uses the death penalty, the only European country to do so.

questions.question: What European country has Brussels as its capital and is known for chocolate, waffles, and beer?
questions.explanation: Capital: Brussels. Population ~11.6 million. Three official languages: Dutch (spoken in Flanders, ~60% of population), French (spoken in Wallonia, ~40%), and German (small community in the east). Predominantly Roman Catholic. Federal parliamentary constitutional monarchy. Belgium gained independence from the Netherlands in 1830. The country has one of the most complex political structures in the world, with six governments serving overlapping linguistic and regional communities. Brussels serves as the de facto capital of the European Union and the headquarters of NATO. Notable figures: Rene Magritte, Peter Paul Rubens, Herge (creator of Tintin), Audrey Hepburn (born in Brussels), Georges Lemaitre (proposed the Big Bang theory), Adolphe Sax (invented the saxophone). Major industries: chemicals, pharmaceuticals, automotive assembly, diamond trading (Antwerp handles 80% of the world's rough diamonds). Belgium has the densest railway network in Europe. The country produces over 220,000 tonnes of chocolate per year. Belgium has more varieties of beer than any country, with over 1,500 distinct beers. The Battle of Waterloo (1815) was fought just south of Brussels. Belgium introduced compulsory voting in 1893.

questions.question: What European country has Sarajevo as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Sarajevo. Population ~3.2 million. Official languages: Bosnian, Croatian, and Serbian (mutually intelligible). Religious makeup mirrors ethnic divisions: Bosniaks (Muslim ~51%), Serbs (Orthodox ~31%), Croats (Catholic ~15%). Federal parliamentary republic with an extraordinarily complex governance structure: a three-member rotating presidency (one Bosniak, one Serb, one Croat). The country has two largely autonomous entities: the Federation of Bosnia and Herzegovina and Republika Srpska. Sarajevo is where Gavrilo Princip assassinated Archduke Franz Ferdinand on June 28, 1914, triggering World War I. The Bosnian War (1992-1995) killed roughly 100,000 people and included the Srebrenica massacre, the worst mass killing in Europe since WWII. Notable figures: Ivo Andric (1961 Nobel Prize in Literature for The Bridge on the Drina), Emir Kusturica (filmmaker), Danis Tanovic (filmmaker, won Oscar for No Man's Land). Sarajevo hosted the 1984 Winter Olympics. The Stari Most (Old Bridge) in Mostar, originally built by the Ottomans in 1566 and destroyed in 1993, was rebuilt and reopened in 2004 as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Bosnia's Mehmed Pasha Sokolovic Bridge is also a UNESCO site. The country has one of Europe's last remaining primeval forests at Perucica.

questions.question: What European country has Sofia as its capital and borders the Black Sea?
questions.explanation: Capital: Sofia. Population ~6.5 million. Official language: Bulgarian, written in Cyrillic script (Bulgaria is credited with creating the Cyrillic alphabet through the disciples of Saints Cyril and Methodius in the 9th century). Predominantly Bulgarian Orthodox Christian (~60%). Parliamentary republic. Bulgaria was founded in 681 AD, making it the oldest country in Europe to have kept its original name continuously. Bulgarians shake their heads to mean 'yes' and nod for 'no,' the opposite of most cultures. Notable figures: Christo (artist known for monumental wrapping installations), Elias Canetti (1981 Nobel Prize in Literature, born in Ruse), Grigor Dimitrov (tennis), Hristo Stoichkov (football). Major industries: machinery, electronics, agriculture (world's top producer of rose oil, grown in the Valley of Roses, supplying 85% of the world's supply, used in perfumery). Bulgaria joined the EU in 2007. The Rila Monastery, founded in the 10th century, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Yogurt culture Lactobacillus bulgaricus is named after Bulgaria, where yogurt has been a dietary staple for millennia. The Thracian tomb of Kazanlak and the ancient gold treasure of Varna (dating to 4600 BC, the oldest processed gold in the world) are major archaeological finds. Bulgaria's Black Sea coast is a major tourist destination.

questions.question: What European country has Zagreb as its capital and a distinctive crescent-shaped coastline on the Adriatic?
questions.explanation: Capital: Zagreb. Population ~3.9 million. Official language: Croatian. Predominantly Roman Catholic (~86%). Parliamentary republic. Croatia declared independence from Yugoslavia in 1991, followed by the Croatian War of Independence (1991-1995). The country joined the EU in 2013 and adopted the euro in 2023. Croatia's Adriatic coastline stretches over 1,100 miles and includes more than 1,200 islands and islets, though fewer than 50 are permanently inhabited. Notable figures: Nikola Tesla (born in Smiljan, Croatia, then part of the Austrian Empire, though also claimed by Serbia), Slavenka Drakulic (writer), Luka Modric (footballer, 2018 Ballon d'Or). Major industries: tourism (Dubrovnik, Split, and Plitvice Lakes), shipbuilding, food processing, pharmaceuticals. The necktie (cravat) was invented in Croatia -- the word 'cravat' derives from 'Croat,' after Croatian mercenaries wore distinctive knotted scarves in the Thirty Years' War. Dubrovnik served as a filming location for King's Landing in Game of Thrones. Plitvice Lakes National Park, a UNESCO site, features 16 terraced lakes connected by waterfalls. The Dalmatian dog breed is named after Croatia's Dalmatia region. Croatia's national sport alongside football is water polo, with multiple Olympic medals.

questions.question: What island nation in the eastern Mediterranean has Nicosia as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Nicosia, the world's last divided capital -- a UN buffer zone (the Green Line) has split it between the Greek Cypriot south and Turkish-controlled north since 1974. Population ~1.2 million (Republic of Cyprus). Official languages: Greek and Turkish. Predominantly Greek Orthodox (~78%) in the south, Sunni Muslim in the north. Presidential republic. Cyprus gained independence from Britain in 1960. In 1974, a Greek-backed coup prompted a Turkish military invasion, dividing the island. The self-declared Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus is recognized only by Turkey. Cyprus is geographically in Asia (closer to Syria than to Greece) but politically European, joining the EU in 2004. Notable figures: Zeno of Citium (founder of Stoic philosophy), Makarios III (first president). Major industries: financial services, tourism, shipping (one of the world's largest ship registries by tonnage). In mythology, Cyprus is the birthplace of Aphrodite, goddess of love, said to have risen from the sea at Petra tou Romiou. Cyprus has been ruled by Assyrians, Egyptians, Persians, Romans, Byzantines, Crusaders, Venetians, Ottomans, and British. The island has extensive copper deposits -- the word 'copper' itself derives from the Latin 'cuprum,' meaning 'from Cyprus.' Halloumi cheese originates from Cyprus.

questions.question: What European country has Prague as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Prague. Population ~10.8 million. Official language: Czech. Historically Catholic but now one of the most secular countries in the world (~35% atheist/agnostic). Parliamentary republic. Czechoslovakia was created in 1918 from territories of the Austro-Hungarian Empire. The country was occupied by Nazi Germany (1938-1945), then became a communist state under Soviet influence. The Prague Spring of 1968 was a brief liberalization crushed by Soviet invasion. The Velvet Revolution of 1989 peacefully ended communist rule. The Czech Republic and Slovakia peacefully separated on January 1, 1993 (the Velvet Divorce). The short name 'Czechia' was officially adopted in 2016. Notable figures: Franz Kafka, Vaclav Havel (playwright and first president), Antonin Dvorak, Bedrich Smetana, Alphonse Mucha, Milan Kundera, Gregor Mendel (father of genetics, worked in Brno), Sigmund Freud (born in Moravia). The Czechs drink more beer per capita than any nation on Earth (~140 liters per person per year). Pilsner beer was invented in the Czech city of Plzen in 1842. Prague's historic center survived WWII largely intact because the Allies chose not to bomb it. Prague Castle is the largest ancient castle complex in the world. The Czech Republic is a major automobile producer (Skoda).

questions.question: What Scandinavian country has Copenhagen as its capital and consists of a peninsula plus over 400 islands?
questions.explanation: Capital: Copenhagen. Population ~5.9 million (Denmark proper). Official language: Danish. Predominantly Lutheran Protestant (~74% belong to the Church of Denmark). Constitutional monarchy and parliamentary democracy. Denmark's monarchy dates to the Viking Age king Gorm the Old (~936 AD), making it the oldest continuous monarchy in Europe. The Kingdom of Denmark includes the Faroe Islands and Greenland (the world's largest island), giving it sovereignty over a vast total area. Notable figures: Hans Christian Andersen, Soren Kierkegaard (father of existentialism), Niels Bohr (Nobel Prize in Physics, atomic model), Tycho Brahe (astronomer), Karen Blixen (Out of Africa), Lars von Trier (filmmaker), Vitus Bering (explorer). Major industries: pharmaceuticals (Novo Nordisk), wind energy (Vestas), shipping (Maersk, the world's largest container shipping company), agriculture (pork, dairy). Denmark is so flat that its highest natural point, Mollehoj, is only 561 feet. The Oresund Bridge connects Copenhagen to Malmo, Sweden. LEGO was invented in Billund, Denmark in 1932 -- the name comes from 'leg godt' (play well). Denmark has the oldest flag in continuous use, the Dannebrog, dating to 1219. Danes pay among the highest taxes in the world but consistently rank among the happiest nations.

questions.question: What is the northernmost of the three Baltic states, with Tallinn as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Tallinn. Population ~1.3 million. Official language: Estonian, a Finno-Ugric language closely related to Finnish and completely unrelated to the Indo-European languages of its Baltic neighbors Latvia and Lithuania. Historically Lutheran, now one of the least religious countries in the world. Parliamentary republic. Estonia declared independence from Russia in 1918, was occupied by the Soviet Union from 1940 to 1991, and regained independence during the Singing Revolution. Joined NATO and the EU in 2004. Estonia is one of the most digitally advanced nations on Earth: citizens can vote online, 99% of government services are available digitally, and in 2014 it launched e-Residency, allowing anyone worldwide to register an EU-based business. Skype was created by Estonian developers in 2003. Notable figures: Arvo Part (classical composer, the most performed living composer globally), Jaan Tallinn (co-founder of Skype), Kerli (singer). Major industries: IT and technology, electronics, timber, food products. Tallinn's Old Town is one of the best-preserved medieval cities in Europe, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Estonia has over 2,000 islands and more than 1,400 lakes. The country has the highest number of startups per capita in Europe. Estonia's national epic is the Kalevipoeg, compiled by Friedrich Reinhold Kreutzwald in the 19th century.

questions.question: What European country has Helsinki as its capital and shares its longest border with Russia?
questions.explanation: Capital: Helsinki. Population ~5.5 million. Official languages: Finnish and Swedish. Predominantly Lutheran (~67%). Parliamentary republic. Finland was part of Sweden for 600 years, then an autonomous Grand Duchy of Russia from 1809 until independence in 1917. Finland fought the Soviet Union in the Winter War (1939-1940), losing 11% of its territory but maintaining independence. The term 'Finlandization' entered political vocabulary to describe a smaller country's accommodation of a powerful neighbor. Finland joined NATO in 2023 after decades of neutrality. Notable figures: Jean Sibelius (composer), Alvar Aalto (architect/designer), Tove Jansson (creator of the Moomins), Linus Torvalds (creator of Linux), Mika Hakkinen and Kimi Raikkonen (Formula 1 champions). Major industries: technology (Nokia), forestry and paper, machinery, shipbuilding. Finland has about 3.3 million saunas for 5.5 million people -- more saunas than cars. There are 188,000 lakes in Finland, earning it the nickname 'Land of a Thousand Lakes' (a massive understatement). Finland consistently ranks first in world happiness and education rankings. Finnish has no grammatical gender and is a Finno-Ugric language, unrelated to Scandinavian languages. Finland was the first European country to grant women full suffrage (1906). The Finns have a concept called 'sisu,' meaning extraordinary determination in the face of adversity.

questions.question: What is the largest country in the European Union by area?
questions.explanation: Capital: Paris. Founded as a unified kingdom in 843 (Treaty of Verdun). Population ~68 million, largest EU country by area at 248,573 square miles (metropolitan France). Official language: French. Predominantly Catholic (~50%), growing secular population. Semi-presidential republic, currently in its Fifth Republic (established by Charles de Gaulle in 1958). Notable figures: Napoleon Bonaparte, Louis XIV, Marie Curie (physics and chemistry Nobel laureate), Victor Hugo, Claude Monet, Claude Debussy, Coco Chanel, Voltaire, Rene Descartes, Louis Pasteur, Albert Camus, Jean-Paul Sartre. Major industries: aerospace (Airbus), luxury goods (LVMH), wine and spirits, nuclear energy (roughly 70% of electricity), tourism, automotive (Renault, Peugeot). France is the world's most visited country with approximately 90 million tourists per year. France spans 12 time zones due to overseas territories (more than any other country, including Russia). Metropolitan France is sometimes called 'l'Hexagone' due to its roughly hexagonal shape. The French Foreign Legion, founded in 1831, accepts recruits of any nationality. The Louvre is the world's most visited museum. The Tour de France has been held since 1903. France has won the FIFA World Cup twice (1998, 2018). France has the most Nobel Prize winners in Literature (16). The Millau Viaduct in southern France is the tallest bridge in the world at 1,125 feet.

questions.question: What European country has Berlin as its capital and is the most populous nation in the EU?
questions.explanation: Capital: Berlin. Population ~84 million, the most populous EU country. Official language: German. Mixed Christian (roughly 25% Catholic, 25% Protestant), with a large secular population (~40%). Federal parliamentary republic consisting of 16 states (Bundeslander). Germany was unified as a nation-state in 1871 under Otto von Bismarck. It was divided into East (GDR) and West (FRG) from 1949 to 1990, with the Berlin Wall (1961-1989) as the most visible symbol of the Cold War. Reunification occurred on October 3, 1990. Notable figures: Johann Sebastian Bach, Ludwig van Beethoven, Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, Albert Einstein, Karl Marx, Martin Luther, Max Planck, Werner Heisenberg, Johannes Gutenberg (printing press), Friedrich Nietzsche. Germany is Europe's largest economy and the world's fourth largest. Major industries: automotive (Volkswagen, BMW, Mercedes-Benz, Porsche), chemicals (BASF, Bayer), engineering, technology (SAP). Germany has over 1,500 breweries and Oktoberfest in Munich draws about 6 million visitors annually. The Autobahn has sections with no speed limit. Germany has the third-highest number of UNESCO World Heritage Sites globally. The country is a world leader in renewable energy, especially solar and wind power. The Berlin Philharmonic is considered one of the finest orchestras in the world.

questions.question: What European country has Athens as its capital and is considered the birthplace of democracy?
questions.explanation: Capital: Athens. Population ~10.4 million. Official language: Greek. Predominantly Greek Orthodox Christian (~90%). Parliamentary republic. Greece is considered the cradle of Western civilization, birthplace of democracy (Athens, 5th century BC), the Olympic Games (776 BC), philosophy, drama, and Western literature. Modern Greece gained independence from the Ottoman Empire in 1829. Notable figures: Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, Homer, Sophocles, Euripides, Archimedes, Hippocrates (father of medicine), El Greco (painter, born in Crete), Nikos Kazantzakis (Zorba the Greek), Nana Mouskouri, Maria Callas (born in New York to Greek parents). Major industries: shipping (Greece has the largest merchant fleet in the world by tonnage), tourism, agriculture (olives, olive oil), food processing. Greece has more archaeological museums than any other country. The Greek alphabet, developed around 800 BC, was the first alphabet to include vowels and is the ancestor of the Latin and Cyrillic alphabets. Greece has approximately 6,000 islands and islets, of which about 227 are inhabited. The country has more coastline than any other Mediterranean country. No point in Greece is more than 85 miles from the sea. Greece hosted the first modern Olympics in Athens in 1896. The Parthenon in Athens was built in 447-432 BC and has influenced architecture worldwide. Greeks consume more olive oil per capita than any other nation.

questions.question: What European country has Budapest as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Budapest. Population ~9.7 million. Official language: Hungarian (Magyar), a Finno-Ugric language completely unrelated to any neighboring language and notoriously difficult to learn. Predominantly Catholic (~37%), Calvinist (~12%), with a large secular population. Parliamentary republic. The Magyars settled in the Carpathian Basin around 896 AD under Arpad. The Kingdom of Hungary was established in 1000 AD when Stephen I was crowned first king. Budapest is actually two cities -- Buda and Pest -- separated by the Danube River, unified in 1873. Notable figures: Franz Liszt (composer), Bela Bartok (composer), Zoltan Kodaly (composer), Laszlo Biro (invented the modern ballpoint pen), Erno Rubik (invented the Rubik's Cube), Dennis Gabor (Nobel Prize for holography), John von Neumann (mathematician), Leo Szilard (nuclear chain reaction), Edward Teller (hydrogen bomb). Hungary has produced 13 Nobel Prize laureates, a remarkable number for a nation of its size. Major industries: automotive manufacturing, pharmaceuticals, electronics, agriculture (paprika, Tokaj wine). Budapest has the largest thermal water cave system in the world and more than 120 hot springs. The Hungarian Parliament Building is one of the largest in the world. Hungary's national dish is goulash, originally a cattle herder's stew. The word 'coach' comes from the Hungarian village of Kocs, where a type of horse-drawn carriage was first made.

questions.question: What European island nation has Reykjavik as its capital, the world's most northerly capital of a sovereign state?
questions.explanation: Capital: Reykjavik. Population ~380,000. Official language: Icelandic, a North Germanic language so conservative that modern Icelanders can still read the medieval sagas written 800 years ago. Predominantly Lutheran (~62%). Parliamentary republic. Iceland was settled by Norse Vikings beginning around 874 AD. The Althing, founded in 930 AD at Thingvellir, is one of the oldest surviving parliaments in the world. Iceland was under Norwegian and then Danish rule until full independence in 1944. Iceland has no standing army and never has; it joined NATO in 1949 on the condition it would not maintain a military. Notable figures: Bjork, Sigur Ros, Halldor Laxness (1955 Nobel Prize in Literature), Leif Erikson (Viking explorer who reached North America ~500 years before Columbus). Major industries: fishing, aluminum smelting (using geothermal and hydroelectric power), tourism. Iceland straddles the Mid-Atlantic Ridge where the North American and Eurasian tectonic plates diverge. About 11% of the country is covered by glaciers, yet it has abundant geothermal energy -- nearly 100% of homes are heated geothermally. Iceland has no mosquitoes. The 2010 eruption of Eyjafjallajokull disrupted European air travel for weeks. Icelanders use patronymic surnames (phone books are sorted by first name). Iceland's genetic homogeneity has made it invaluable for medical genetics research.

questions.question: What European country has Dublin as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Dublin. Population ~5.1 million. Official languages: Irish (Gaelic) and English. Predominantly Roman Catholic (~78%), though church influence has declined significantly. Parliamentary republic. Ireland gained partial independence from Britain as the Irish Free State in 1922 after the War of Independence, and became a fully sovereign republic in 1949. The Great Famine (1845-1852) killed about one million people and caused another million to emigrate, primarily to the US. Ireland's population peaked at about 8.2 million in 1841 and only recently surpassed 5 million again. More people of Irish descent live abroad (especially the US, with ~33 million claiming Irish ancestry) than in Ireland. Notable figures: James Joyce, Oscar Wilde, W.B. Yeats (Nobel Prize in Literature 1923), Samuel Beckett (Nobel Prize 1969), Seamus Heaney (Nobel Prize 1995), George Bernard Shaw (Nobel Prize 1925), Bono and U2, Sinead O'Connor, Enya. Major industries: pharmaceuticals (9 of the top 10 pharma companies operate there), technology (European headquarters of Apple, Google, Meta, Microsoft), agriculture, food and beverages (Guinness, Jameson). Ireland's 12.5% corporate tax rate transformed it into the 'Celtic Tiger' economy. The island has no snakes, popularly attributed to St. Patrick. Ireland was the first country to legalize same-sex marriage by popular vote (2015). The harp, not the shamrock, is Ireland's official national symbol.

questions.question: What European country shaped like a boot has Rome as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Rome. Population ~59 million. Official language: Italian. Predominantly Roman Catholic (~80%), home of the Vatican. Parliamentary republic since 1946 (monarchy abolished by referendum). Italy was unified as a modern nation-state in 1861 during the Risorgimento, led by figures like Giuseppe Garibaldi, Camillo di Cavour, and Giuseppe Mazzini. Italy contains two fully independent microstates within its borders: Vatican City and San Marino. Notable figures: Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, Galileo Galilei, Dante Alighieri, Marco Polo, Giuseppe Verdi, Enrico Fermi (nuclear physics), Guglielmo Marconi (radio), Federico Fellini, Sophia Loren. Major industries: luxury goods (Gucci, Prada, Ferrari, Lamborghini), machinery, automotive (Fiat), food and wine, tourism. Italy has the most UNESCO World Heritage Sites of any country in the world (59). The Roman Empire, centered in Italy, shaped Western civilization for centuries. Italy has more varieties of cheese than France. The University of Bologna, founded in 1088, is the oldest university in continuous operation. Italians invented pizza, espresso, the piano, the barometer, and the battery (Volta). Italy has active volcanoes: Vesuvius, Etna, and Stromboli. The Italian economy is the third-largest in the EU. Italy has had more than 60 governments since WWII.

questions.question: What European country declared independence from Serbia in 2008, with Pristina as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Pristina. Population ~1.8 million. Official languages: Albanian and Serbian. Predominantly Muslim (~96%), mostly ethnic Albanian. Parliamentary republic. Kosovo is Europe's youngest country, having declared independence from Serbia on February 17, 2008. Its independence is recognized by over 100 UN member states (including the US, UK, France, and Germany) but not by Serbia, Russia, China, Spain, or Greece. Kosovo was the site of the Battle of Kosovo in 1389, a pivotal clash between Serbian and Ottoman forces that holds deep symbolic significance for Serbs. The Kosovo War of 1998-1999 ended with NATO's bombing campaign against Serbia, and Kosovo was placed under UN administration. Notable figures: Ibrahim Rugova (first president, 'Gandhi of the Balkans'), Rita Ora (singer, born in Pristina), Dua Lipa (singer, parents are Kosovar Albanian). Kosovo has one of the youngest populations in Europe, with a median age around 30. Major industries: mining (lead, zinc, lignite), construction, remittances from the diaspora. Kosovo is roughly the size of Connecticut. The Decani Monastery, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, dates to the 14th century. Kosovo's economy is one of the poorest in Europe, with high unemployment. Despite its small size, Kosovo has significant mineral wealth, including one of the largest lignite reserves in Europe.

questions.question: What is the middle of the three Baltic states, with Riga as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Riga. Population ~1.8 million. Official language: Latvian, a Baltic language related to Lithuanian (the only two surviving Baltic languages). Predominantly Lutheran and Catholic, with a significant Orthodox minority. Parliamentary republic. Latvia declared independence from Russia in 1918, was occupied by the Soviet Union from 1940 to 1991, and regained independence during the Singing Revolution. Joined NATO and the EU in 2004. Riga has the largest and best-preserved collection of Art Nouveau (Jugendstil) architecture in the world, with over 800 buildings in the style, primarily designed by Mikhail Eisenstein (father of filmmaker Sergei Eisenstein). Latvia's flag -- dark red (maroon) with a white horizontal stripe -- is one of the oldest in the world, first mentioned in the medieval Livonian Rhymed Chronicle of 1280. Notable figures: Mikhail Baryshnikov (ballet dancer, born in Riga), Gidon Kremer (violinist), Isaiah Berlin (philosopher, born in Riga), Mark Rothko (painter, born in Daugavpils). Major industries: timber and wood processing, food products, metals, pharmaceuticals, IT. About half of Latvia is forested. The Daugava River is the country's primary waterway. Latvia has one of the fastest population declines in the EU due to emigration and low birth rates. Riga was the largest city in the Swedish Empire during the 17th century. The Latvian Song and Dance Festival, held every five years, features up to 40,000 performers.

questions.question: What tiny European country lies between Austria and Switzerland, with Vaduz as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Vaduz. Population ~39,000. Official language: German. Predominantly Roman Catholic (~73%). Constitutional monarchy (principality) under the House of Liechtenstein. The country was created in 1719 within the Holy Roman Empire and gained full sovereignty in 1866. Liechtenstein is doubly landlocked -- surrounded entirely by other landlocked countries (Austria and Switzerland), one of only two such countries in the world (the other is Uzbekistan). Area: only 62 square miles, roughly the size of Washington, D.C. Despite its tiny size, it has the highest GDP per capita in the world when adjusted for purchasing power, largely due to its financial services sector and low taxes. The country uses the Swiss franc as its currency and relies on Switzerland for defense. Notable figures: the princely House of Liechtenstein itself is one of the wealthiest royal families in Europe, with an art collection valued in the billions. Major industries: financial services, ceramics, dental products (Ivoclar Vivadent), precision instruments. Liechtenstein is the world's largest producer of false teeth. The country has no airport, no military (abolished in 1868 after its last deployment of 80 soldiers returned with 81 men -- they made a friend), and no national debt. Liechtenstein was the last country in Europe to grant women the right to vote (1984). It has won more Olympic medals per capita than any other country, all in Alpine skiing.

questions.question: What is the southernmost of the three Baltic states, with Vilnius as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Vilnius. Population ~2.8 million. Official language: Lithuanian, a Baltic language and one of the oldest surviving Indo-European languages, retaining many archaic features. Predominantly Roman Catholic (~77%). Parliamentary republic. Lithuania declared independence from the Soviet Union on March 11, 1990, the first Soviet republic to do so. Joined NATO and the EU in 2004. In the 15th century, the Grand Duchy of Lithuania was one of the largest states in Europe, stretching from the Baltic Sea to the Black Sea. Lithuania and Poland united in the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (1569-1795), one of Europe's largest and most diverse states. Notable figures: Czeslaw Milosz (1980 Nobel Prize in Literature, born in Lithuania), Jonas Mekas (filmmaker, 'godfather of American avant-garde cinema'), Romain Gary (French novelist, born in Vilnius), Jascha Heifetz (violinist, born in Vilnius). Major industries: lasers (Lithuania is one of the world's top producers of high-precision lasers), furniture, food processing, IT. The Hill of Crosses near Siauliai contains over 100,000 crosses and is a site of Catholic pilgrimage and national defiance (the Soviets bulldozed it multiple times but it was always rebuilt). Basketball is essentially the national religion -- Lithuania has won three Olympic bronze medals and multiple European championships. Vilnius's Old Town is one of the largest in Eastern Europe and a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

questions.question: What small European country is bordered by Belgium, France, and Germany, and shares its name with its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Luxembourg City. Population ~660,000. Three official languages: Luxembourgish (a Moselle Franconian dialect of German), French, and German. Most residents speak all three. Predominantly Roman Catholic (~68%). Constitutional monarchy (Grand Duchy -- the world's only remaining sovereign grand duchy). Luxembourg was a founding member of the EU (then EEC), NATO, and the United Nations. It has the highest GDP per capita in the world (nominal), driven largely by financial services -- the country is home to over 140 banks and is the second-largest investment fund center globally after the US. About 47% of the workforce commutes from neighboring countries (France, Belgium, Germany), one of the highest cross-border commuter rates anywhere. Notable figures: Robert Schuman (founding father of the EU, born in Luxembourg), Hugo Gernsback (father of science fiction publishing), Edward Steichen (photographer). Major industries: financial services, steel (historically -- ArcelorMittal's predecessor was founded here), technology, logistics. Luxembourg City's old quarters and fortifications are a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The country is only 998 square miles, roughly the size of Rhode Island. Luxembourg was occupied by Germany in both World Wars despite its declared neutrality. The European Court of Justice and other key EU institutions are based in Luxembourg. The country has one of the highest standards of living in the world. Luxembourg consumes more alcohol per capita than any EU country, partly due to cross-border shoppers buying cheaper fuel and alcohol.

questions.question: What small European island nation lies south of Sicily in the Mediterranean, with Valletta as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Valletta, the smallest national capital in the EU by area (less than 0.25 square miles), and a UNESCO World Heritage Site in its entirety. Population ~520,000. Official languages: Maltese (the only Semitic language written in Latin script, with heavy Italian and English influence) and English. Predominantly Roman Catholic (~88%). Parliamentary republic. Malta gained independence from Britain in 1964 and joined the EU in 2004. The islands have been inhabited since about 5900 BC. The Megalithic Temples of Malta (including Ggantija on Gozo) predate the Egyptian pyramids by about 1,000 years, making them among the oldest free-standing structures on Earth. The Knights of St. John (Knights Hospitaller) ruled Malta from 1530 to 1798 and built Valletta's famous fortifications. Malta withstood the Great Siege by the Ottoman Empire in 1565. During WWII, Malta was one of the most heavily bombed places on Earth; King George VI awarded the entire island the George Cross, which now appears on Malta's flag. Notable figures: Daphne Caruana Galizia (investigative journalist), Caravaggio (lived and painted in Malta 1607-1608). Major industries: tourism, financial services, online gaming (iGaming hub of Europe), film production. Malta has no rivers, lakes, or mountains. The archipelago consists of three inhabited islands: Malta, Gozo, and Comino (famous for the Blue Lagoon).

questions.question: What European country has Chisinau as its capital and is landlocked between Romania and Ukraine?
questions.explanation: Capital: Chisinau. Population ~2.6 million. Official language: Romanian (constitutionally called 'Moldovan' until 2023, but linguistically identical to Romanian). Predominantly Eastern Orthodox Christian (~90%). Parliamentary republic. Moldova declared independence from the Soviet Union in 1991. The region of Bessarabia, which makes up most of modern Moldova, was historically part of the Principality of Moldavia, then traded between the Ottoman, Russian, and Romanian empires. Moldova has a breakaway region called Transnistria (along the Ukrainian border) that operates as a de facto independent state with its own government, currency, and border controls, propped up by Russian troops stationed there since 1992. Notable figures: few internationally well-known, though violinist Patricia Kopatchinskaja was born in Chisinau. Major industries: agriculture (wine, fruit, vegetables, tobacco), textiles, food processing. Moldova is one of the poorest countries in Europe. However, it is a major wine producer -- wine accounts for a significant share of exports, and the Milestii Mici wine cellar holds the Guinness record for the world's largest collection of wine (over 2 million bottles). The cellars stretch for 120 miles of underground tunnels in former limestone mines. Moldova is the least-visited country in Europe by tourist arrivals. The country has applied for EU membership, with candidate status granted in 2022. A significant portion of the population has emigrated for work, and remittances form a major part of GDP.

questions.question: What is the smallest European country by area after Vatican City, located on the French Riviera?
questions.explanation: Capital: Monaco (city-state, with Monte Carlo as its most famous district). Population ~39,000. Official language: French. Predominantly Roman Catholic (~86%). Constitutional monarchy (principality) under the House of Grimaldi, which has ruled since 1297, making it one of the oldest ruling families in Europe. Area: only 0.78 square miles (2.02 sq km), smaller than Central Park in New York City. Monaco is the most densely populated sovereign country in the world. It has no income tax, no capital gains tax, and no wealth tax, making it a magnet for the ultra-wealthy -- about one-third of residents are millionaires. Notable figures: Prince Rainier III (who married Hollywood actress Grace Kelly in 1956), Princess Grace (Grace Kelly), Prince Albert II (IOC member and environmental activist). The Monaco Grand Prix, held annually since 1929, is one of the most prestigious Formula 1 races and runs through the city streets. The Monte Carlo Casino, opened in 1863, saved the principality from bankruptcy and established its reputation for glamour. Monaco's citizens (Monegasques) are forbidden from entering the casino. Major industries: tourism, banking, real estate, and the Monaco Grand Prix. Monaco has expanded its territory by about 20% through land reclamation from the Mediterranean. Monaco's military consists of about 250 people (the Carabiniers du Prince and the Company of Firemen). Monaco has the highest life expectancy in the world, over 85 years.

questions.question: What European country has Podgorica as its capital and has a name meaning 'Black Mountain'?
questions.explanation: Capital: Podgorica (administrative), though the historic capital and seat of the president is Cetinje. Population ~620,000. Official language: Montenegrin (closely related to Serbian, Bosnian, and Croatian). Predominantly Serbian Orthodox Christian (~72%), with a significant Muslim minority (~19%). Parliamentary republic. Montenegro gained independence from the State Union of Serbia and Montenegro on June 3, 2006, after a referendum that passed by just 0.5% above the required 55% threshold (55.5% voted yes). The name Crna Gora (Black Mountain) has been used since the medieval era. Montenegro was one of the few Balkan territories that was never fully conquered by the Ottoman Empire, maintaining a form of autonomy from its mountain strongholds. Notable figures: King Nikola I (last king), Emir Kusturica (filmmaker, sometimes claimed by Bosnia and Serbia). Major industries: tourism (the Adriatic coast, particularly Kotor and Budva), aluminum, agriculture, energy. The Bay of Kotor is often called 'Europe's southernmost fjord' (though technically a submerged river canyon), and the old town of Kotor is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Durmitor National Park is also a UNESCO site, featuring the Tara River Canyon, the deepest canyon in Europe and the second deepest in the world after the Grand Canyon. Montenegro uses the euro despite not being in the EU or eurozone. The country is a NATO member since 2017 and an EU candidate country.

questions.question: What European country has Amsterdam as its capital and is famous for its windmills, tulips, and extensive canal system?
questions.explanation: Capital: Amsterdam (constitutional), though The Hague is the seat of government, parliament, and the International Court of Justice. Population ~17.8 million. Official language: Dutch. Historically Protestant (Calvinist), now largely secular. Constitutional monarchy and parliamentary democracy. The Netherlands declared independence from Spain in 1581, and the Dutch Golden Age (17th century) saw the country become the world's foremost maritime and economic power. About 26% of the Netherlands lies below sea level, and about one-third would be flooded without the country's elaborate system of dikes, sea barriers, and polders. The Delta Works flood protection system is considered one of the Seven Wonders of the Modern World. 'Holland' technically refers to only two of twelve provinces (North and South Holland). Notable figures: Rembrandt van Rijn, Vincent van Gogh, Johannes Vermeer, Baruch Spinoza, Erasmus, Christiaan Huygens, Anne Frank (diarist, born in Germany but lived in Amsterdam). Major industries: agriculture (second-largest agricultural exporter in the world by value despite its small size), chemicals (Shell, AkzoNobel), electronics (Philips, ASML -- maker of the lithography machines essential to all advanced chip manufacturing), logistics (Rotterdam is Europe's largest port). The Dutch are the tallest people in the world on average. Tulips are not native to the Netherlands -- they were imported from the Ottoman Empire, and 'Tulip Mania' in 1637 is often cited as the first economic bubble. The Netherlands was the first country to legalize same-sex marriage (2001).

questions.question: What European country has Skopje as its capital and changed its name in 2019 to resolve a dispute with Greece?
questions.explanation: Capital: Skopje. Population ~1.8 million. Official languages: Macedonian and Albanian. Predominantly Macedonian Orthodox Christian (~65%), with a significant Muslim minority (~33%, mainly ethnic Albanians). Parliamentary republic. The country declared independence from Yugoslavia in 1991 as the 'Republic of Macedonia.' Greece objected because it has its own region called Macedonia (and the ancient Macedonian kingdom of Alexander the Great). This 27-year naming dispute blocked the country from NATO and EU accession. The Prespa Agreement of 2018, implemented in 2019, changed the name to North Macedonia, opening the path to NATO membership (joined 2020). Notable figures: Mother Teresa (born Anjeze Gonxhe Bojaxhiu in Skopje in 1910 to Albanian parents), Toše Proeski (singer, 'Elvis of the Balkans'). The 1963 Skopje earthquake destroyed much of the city; it was partially redesigned by Japanese architect Kenzo Tange. Major industries: automotive parts, textiles, agriculture (tobacco, wine), food processing. Lake Ohrid, shared with Albania, is one of Europe's oldest and deepest lakes, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, home to unique endemic species. North Macedonia's 'Skopje 2014' project controversially erected hundreds of statues and neoclassical buildings to project historical grandeur, drawing criticism for historical revisionism and cost overruns. The country is about the size of Vermont.

questions.question: What Scandinavian country has Oslo as its capital and is famous for its fjords?
questions.explanation: Capital: Oslo. Population ~5.5 million. Official languages: Norwegian (two written forms: Bokmal and Nynorsk) and Sami. Predominantly Lutheran (~68%). Constitutional monarchy and parliamentary democracy. Norway gained independence from Sweden in 1905. The country's vast North Sea and Arctic oil and gas reserves, discovered in the late 1960s, transformed it into one of the world's wealthiest nations. Norway manages the Government Pension Fund Global (commonly called the Oil Fund), the world's largest sovereign wealth fund, worth over $1.5 trillion, owning roughly 1.5% of all listed stocks globally. Notable figures: Edvard Grieg (composer), Henrik Ibsen (playwright, father of modern drama), Edvard Munch (painter, The Scream), Roald Amundsen (first to reach the South Pole, 1911), Thor Heyerdahl (Kon-Tiki expedition), Fridtjof Nansen (explorer, Nobel Peace Prize), Liv Ullmann (actress). Major industries: petroleum and natural gas, seafood (salmon farming), shipping, hydroelectric power. Norway's coastline, including fjords, stretches over 63,000 miles. The country extends north of the Arctic Circle, and the northern city of Tromso experiences midnight sun in summer and polar night in winter. Norway is not a member of the EU (rejected membership by referendum in 1972 and 1994) but participates in the European Economic Area. The Nobel Peace Prize is awarded in Oslo (all other Nobel Prizes are awarded in Stockholm).

questions.question: What European country has Warsaw as its capital and lies between Germany and Ukraine?
questions.explanation: Capital: Warsaw. Population ~38 million, the fifth most populous EU country. Official language: Polish. Predominantly Roman Catholic (~87%), one of the most devoutly Catholic countries in Europe. Parliamentary republic. Poland was erased from the map for 123 years (1795-1918), partitioned between Russia, Prussia, and Austria, yet Polish language and culture survived. Poland was reconstituted after WWI but suffered tremendously in WWII: invaded by both Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union in 1939, it lost roughly six million citizens, half of them Jewish (the Holocaust decimated Poland's pre-war Jewish population of 3.3 million). Warsaw was almost entirely destroyed in WWII, then meticulously rebuilt. Notable figures: Frederic Chopin (composer), Marie Curie (born Maria Sklodowska), Nicolaus Copernicus, Pope John Paul II (Karol Wojtyla, first non-Italian pope in 455 years), Lech Walesa (Solidarity movement, Nobel Peace Prize 1983), Wislawa Szymborska (Nobel Prize in Literature 1996), Andrzej Wajda (filmmaker). Major industries: automotive, machinery, electronics, food processing, IT. Poland is the largest economy in Central Europe. The Wieliczka Salt Mine near Krakow, in operation since the 13th century, includes underground chambers, a chapel, and sculptures all carved from rock salt, and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Bialowieza Forest (shared with Belarus) is one of Europe's last primeval forests, home to the European bison. Poland is the world's largest amber exporter.

questions.question: What European country has Lisbon as its capital and occupies the western edge of the Iberian Peninsula?
questions.explanation: Capital: Lisbon. Population ~10.3 million. Official language: Portuguese (spoken by over 250 million people worldwide, mainly in Brazil). Predominantly Roman Catholic (~81%). Semi-presidential republic. Portugal's borders have been essentially unchanged since 1249, making it one of the oldest nation-states in Europe. The Treaty of Windsor (1386) between Portugal and England is the oldest active alliance in the world. Portugal's Age of Exploration in the 15th-16th centuries, pioneered by Henry the Navigator, Vasco da Gama (first European to reach India by sea, 1498), and Pedro Alvares Cabral (reached Brazil, 1500), created a global empire spanning Brazil, Angola, Mozambique, Goa, Macau, and Timor-Leste. Notable figures: Fernando Pessoa (modernist poet), Jose Saramago (1998 Nobel Prize in Literature), Amalia Rodrigues (fado singer), Eusebio (footballer), Cristiano Ronaldo. Major industries: tourism, textiles, footwear, cork (Portugal produces about half the world's cork), wine (Port wine from the Douro Valley), automotive parts, renewable energy. Portugal decriminalized the personal use of all drugs in 2001, a policy widely studied as a model. The 1755 Lisbon earthquake, one of the deadliest in history, destroyed much of the city and profoundly influenced Enlightenment philosophy (notably Voltaire's Candide). Fado, the melancholic Portuguese music genre, is a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. The Vasco da Gama Bridge in Lisbon is the longest bridge in Europe at 10.7 miles.

questions.question: What European country has Bucharest as its capital and is home to the Transylvania region?
questions.explanation: Capital: Bucharest. Population ~19 million. Official language: Romanian, a Romance language descended from Latin, the only such language in Eastern Europe, surrounded by Slavic and Hungarian-speaking neighbors. Predominantly Romanian Orthodox Christian (~81%). Semi-presidential republic. Romania gained full independence from the Ottoman Empire in 1877-1878 and unified with Transylvania, Banat, and Bukovina in 1918. The country endured a harsh communist regime under Nicolae Ceausescu (1965-1989), who was overthrown and executed in the Romanian Revolution of December 1989. Notable figures: Constantin Brancusi (sculptor, The Kiss, Bird in Space), Eugene Ionesco (playwright, father of absurdist theater), Mircea Eliade (historian of religion), Elie Wiesel (Nobel Peace Prize, born in Sighet), Nadia Comaneci (first gymnast to score a perfect 10 at the Olympics, 1976), Gheorghe Hagi (footballer). Major industries: automotive (Dacia, owned by Renault), IT, agriculture, energy. The Palace of the Parliament in Bucharest is the world's heaviest building and the second-largest administrative building after the Pentagon, built by Ceausescu at enormous cost. Transylvania is a real place -- Bram Stoker set Dracula there, loosely inspired by Vlad the Impaler (Vlad Tepes), a 15th-century Wallachian prince. Romania has the largest population of brown bears in Europe outside Russia. The Danube Delta, where the river meets the Black Sea, is Europe's best-preserved delta and a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve.

questions.question: What is the largest country in Europe by area, with Moscow as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Moscow. Population ~144 million total, with about 110 million living in the European portion. Official language: Russian, written in Cyrillic script. Predominantly Russian Orthodox Christian (~71%). Federal semi-presidential republic. Russia spans both Europe and Asia across 11 time zones, covering 6.6 million square miles (the world's largest country). Roughly 23% of its territory lies in Europe, but even that European portion alone is larger than any entirely European country. The Russian Empire was founded by Tsar Peter the Great, and the Soviet Union (1922-1991) was a superpower that rivaled the United States. Notable figures: Leo Tolstoy, Fyodor Dostoevsky, Anton Chekhov, Alexander Pushkin, Pyotr Tchaikovsky, Igor Stravinsky, Dmitri Mendeleev (periodic table), Yuri Gagarin (first human in space, 1961), Mikhail Gorbachev, Andrei Sakharov (nuclear physicist, Nobel Peace Prize). Major industries: oil and natural gas (one of the world's largest producers), mining, military equipment, aerospace. Lake Baikal in Siberia is the world's deepest and oldest freshwater lake, containing about 20% of the world's unfrozen surface freshwater. The Moscow Metro is famous for its ornate, palace-like stations. The Trans-Siberian Railway (5,772 miles) is the longest railway line in the world. Russia has more forest area than any other country. Russia has won the most Olympic medals in chess's history and produced world champions like Kasparov, Karpov, and Kramnik.

questions.question: What microstate is completely surrounded by Italy and claims to be the world's oldest republic?
questions.explanation: Capital: City of San Marino. Population ~34,000. Official language: Italian. Predominantly Roman Catholic. Parliamentary republic with two Captains Regent serving as joint heads of state for six-month terms, elected by the Grand and General Council. San Marino claims to have been founded on September 3, 301 AD, by a Christian stonemason named Marinus fleeing Roman Emperor Diocletian's persecution, making it the world's oldest surviving republic and oldest constitutional republic. Area: only 24 square miles, the fifth-smallest country in the world, entirely surrounded by Italy (enclaved). San Marino sits atop Monte Titano in the Apennine Mountains, with its three iconic fortress towers visible from miles away. The country has more registered vehicles than people. Napoleon reportedly offered to enlarge San Marino's territory, but the republic wisely declined, preferring to stay small and independent. San Marino has the oldest written constitution still in effect (the Statutes of 1600, with roots in the Leges Statutae of 1263). Major industries: tourism, banking, ceramics, electronics, wine, cheese. San Marino is not a member of the EU but uses the euro by agreement and issues its own euro coins, which are prized by collectors. San Marino has won no Olympic medals despite competing since 1960. It has one of the highest GDP per capita in the world. Abraham Lincoln was granted honorary citizenship of San Marino in 1861.

questions.question: What European country has Belgrade as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Belgrade, meaning 'White City,' situated at the strategic confluence of the Danube and Sava rivers. Population ~6.7 million. Official language: Serbian (written in both Cyrillic and Latin scripts, one of the few languages to use both). Predominantly Serbian Orthodox Christian (~85%). Parliamentary republic. Belgrade is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in Europe, with settlements dating back over 7,000 years. Serbia was the core of the medieval Serbian Empire under Stefan Dusan (14th century), then fell under Ottoman rule for nearly 500 years until gaining autonomy in the 19th century. Serbia was part of Yugoslavia from 1918 until its dissolution in the 1990s-2000s. The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo (then in Austria-Hungary) in 1914 was carried out by Gavrilo Princip, a Bosnian Serb, triggering WWI. Notable figures: Nikola Tesla (born in modern-day Croatia to Serbian parents, claimed by both nations), Novak Djokovic (tennis champion, one of the greatest players in history), Emir Kusturica (filmmaker), Ivo Andric (Nobel Laureate, born in Bosnia, wrote in Serbian). Major industries: automotive (Fiat Chrysler plant), agriculture, food processing, mining, IT. Serbia is one of the largest exporters of raspberries in the world, producing about one-third of the global supply. The EXIT music festival in Novi Sad is one of Europe's largest. Serbia is an EU candidate country but also maintains close ties with Russia and China.

questions.question: What European country has Bratislava as its capital, the only capital that borders two other countries?
questions.explanation: Capital: Bratislava, uniquely situated on the borders of both Austria and Hungary -- the only national capital bordering two sovereign states. Bratislava is only 34 miles from Vienna, making them the two closest national capitals in Europe. Population ~5.4 million. Official language: Slovak. Predominantly Roman Catholic (~62%). Parliamentary republic. Slovakia peacefully separated from the Czech Republic on January 1, 1993, in the 'Velvet Divorce.' The region was part of the Kingdom of Hungary for nearly 1,000 years; Bratislava (then called Pressburg/Pozsony) served as the Hungarian capital from 1536 to 1783 and the coronation city for Hungarian monarchs. Notable figures: Andy Warhol's parents were Slovak immigrants from the village of Mikova. Stefan Banic, a Slovak inventor, patented an early parachute design. Alexander Dubcek led the 1968 Prague Spring liberalization movement. Peter Sagan (cyclist, three-time world champion). Major industries: automotive manufacturing (Slovakia produces more cars per capita than any country in the world, with plants for Volkswagen, Kia, Peugeot, and Jaguar Land Rover), electronics, steel. The country has extensive cave systems, with over 7,000 caves and 13 open to the public. The High Tatras are the smallest alpine mountain range in the world. Slovakia has more castles and chateaux per capita than any other country. Slovak is mutually intelligible with Czech to a significant degree.

questions.question: What European country has Ljubljana as its capital and lies between Italy, Austria, Hungary, and Croatia?
questions.explanation: Capital: Ljubljana. Population ~2.1 million. Official language: Slovenian. Predominantly Roman Catholic (~58%). Parliamentary republic. Slovenia declared independence from Yugoslavia on June 25, 1991, and fought a brief Ten-Day War before the Yugoslav army withdrew. It was the first former Yugoslav republic to join the EU (2004) and the first to adopt the euro (2007). Notable figures: Slavoj Zizek (philosopher), Luka Doncic (NBA basketball star), Melania Trump (former US First Lady, born Melanija Knavs in Novo Mesto), France Preseren (national poet, whose verse appears in the national anthem), Jože Plecnik (architect who redesigned much of Ljubljana). Major industries: automotive parts, pharmaceuticals, machinery, tourism, logistics. Slovenia is one of the most biodiverse countries in Europe, with over 60% forest cover (the third-most forested country in Europe after Finland and Sweden). It has only 29 miles of Adriatic coastline. Lake Bled, with its island church and clifftop castle, is the country's most iconic image. The Postojna Cave system stretches for 15 miles and contains the olm (proteus), a blind cave salamander sometimes called the 'baby dragon.' The Lipizzan horse breed was developed at the Lipica stud farm in Slovenia, now the oldest continuously operating stud farm in Europe (since 1580). Slovenians have the highest rate of beekeeping per capita in the EU.

questions.question: What European country has Madrid as its capital and occupies most of the Iberian Peninsula?
questions.explanation: Capital: Madrid. Population ~48 million. Official language: Spanish (Castilian), with co-official regional languages including Catalan, Galician, Basque, and Aranese. Predominantly Roman Catholic (~56%). Parliamentary constitutional monarchy. Modern Spain was forged through the marriage of Ferdinand of Aragon and Isabella of Castile in 1469 and the completion of the Reconquista (expulsion of the Moors) in 1492 -- the same year Columbus reached the Americas. The Spanish Empire was one of the largest in history. Notable figures: Pablo Picasso, Salvador Dali, Francisco Goya, Diego Velazquez, Miguel de Cervantes (Don Quixote, considered the first modern novel), Federico Garcia Lorca, Antoni Gaudi, Santiago Ramon y Cajal (Nobel Prize in Medicine, father of modern neuroscience), Rafael Nadal. Major industries: tourism (second most visited country in the world), automotive, agriculture (world's largest olive oil producer), renewable energy, pharmaceuticals. Spain has the third-most UNESCO World Heritage Sites globally. The Basque language (Euskara) is a language isolate unrelated to any other known language. Spain's daily schedule runs roughly two hours later than the rest of Europe because Franco changed the time zone to align with Nazi Germany in 1940, and it was never changed back. The Sagrada Familia in Barcelona has been under construction since 1882. Spain won the FIFA World Cup in 2010 and the European Championship three times (1964, 2008, 2012). La Tomatina is an annual tomato-throwing festival in Bunol.

questions.question: What Scandinavian country has Stockholm as its capital and is the largest Nordic country by area?
questions.explanation: Capital: Stockholm. Population ~10.5 million. Official language: Swedish. Predominantly Lutheran (~57% belong to the Church of Sweden). Constitutional monarchy and parliamentary democracy. Sweden was a major European power in the 17th and 18th centuries under kings like Gustavus Adolphus and Charles XII. Sweden has not fought in a foreign war since 1814, maintaining over 200 years of peace, though it joined NATO in 2024, ending its long tradition of military non-alignment. Notable figures: Alfred Nobel (inventor of dynamite, established the Nobel Prizes), Ingmar Bergman (filmmaker), August Strindberg (playwright), Carl Linnaeus (father of modern taxonomy), Greta Garbo, ABBA, Ingrid Bergman (actress, no relation to Ingmar), Astrid Lindgren (Pippi Longstocking), Greta Thunberg, Dag Hammarskjold (UN Secretary-General). Major industries: automotive (Volvo, Scania), telecommunications (Ericsson), music/streaming (Spotify, Sweden is the world's third-largest music exporter after the US and UK), furniture (IKEA), defense (Saab), pharmaceuticals. Stockholm is built on 14 islands connected by 57 bridges. Sweden's Right of Public Access (Allemansratten) allows anyone to walk, camp, and forage on private land. Sweden introduced the world's first central bank (Riksbank, 1668). The country processes less than 1% of its household waste in landfills, using the rest for recycling and energy. The Nobel Prizes (except Peace) are awarded in Stockholm.

questions.question: What European country has Bern as its seat of government and is famous for neutrality, banking, and Alpine scenery?
questions.explanation: Capital: Bern (federal city/seat of government; Switzerland has no official capital). Population ~8.8 million. Four official languages: German (~63%), French (~23%), Italian (~8%), and Romansh (~0.5%). Mixed Christian (Catholic ~34%, Protestant ~22%), with growing secular population. Federal republic with a unique system of direct democracy (citizens vote on national referendums multiple times per year). The Swiss Confederation was founded in 1291 (the oldest surviving federal state). Switzerland has not fought in a foreign war since 1515 (Battle of Marignano). It is not a member of the EU or NATO but hosts the European headquarters of the United Nations in Geneva, the International Red Cross (founded by Swiss Henry Dunant in 1863), FIFA, the IOC, WHO, WTO, and CERN (where the World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989). Notable figures: Albert Einstein (developed special relativity while working in Bern), Carl Jung, Jean-Jacques Rousseau, Le Corbusier, Paul Klee, Roger Federer. Major industries: pharmaceuticals (Novartis, Roche), banking and financial services, watchmaking (Rolex, Swatch, Patek Philippe), machinery, chocolate (Nestle, Lindt, Toblerone), food (Nestle is the world's largest food company). Switzerland has enough nuclear bunker capacity to shelter its entire population. Swiss men are still required to keep military-issued weapons at home. The Gotthard Base Tunnel (35.5 miles) is the world's longest and deepest railway tunnel.

questions.question: What is the largest country located entirely within Europe, with Kyiv as its capital?
questions.explanation: Capital: Kyiv (historically also transliterated as 'Kiev'). Population ~37 million (pre-2022, reduced by war and displacement). Official language: Ukrainian. Predominantly Orthodox Christian (split between multiple churches). Semi-presidential republic. Ukraine is the largest country located entirely in Europe at 233,000 square miles, roughly the size of Texas. It declared independence from the Soviet Union on August 24, 1991. In 2014, Russia annexed Crimea and backed separatists in eastern Ukraine. In February 2022, Russia launched a full-scale invasion. Notable figures: Taras Shevchenko (national poet and artist), Mykola Lysenko (composer, 'father of Ukrainian music'), Sergei Prokofiev (born in Donetsk region), Milla Jovovich (actress, born in Kyiv), the Klitschko brothers (boxing champions, Vitali became mayor of Kyiv). Ukraine's fertile black soil (chernozem) makes it one of the world's most productive agricultural regions, historically earning the nickname 'breadbasket of Europe.' Major industries: agriculture (wheat, corn, sunflower oil -- world's largest sunflower oil exporter), steel, aerospace, IT services. The Chernobyl nuclear disaster of 1986, the worst in history, occurred in northern Ukraine. Kyiv is one of Europe's oldest cities, founded in the 5th century. The Kyiv Pechersk Lavra (Monastery of the Caves) is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Ukraine has won the Eurovision Song Contest three times (2004, 2016, 2022). Borscht, the beetroot soup, is a quintessential Ukrainian dish (recognized by UNESCO as Ukrainian intangible cultural heritage in 2022).

questions.question: What European country has London as its capital and is made up of England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland?
questions.explanation: Capital: London. Population ~67 million. Official language: English, with Welsh, Scottish Gaelic, and Irish also recognized. Predominantly Christian (Anglican/Church of England ~25%), with a large secular population (~37%). Constitutional monarchy and parliamentary democracy. The United Kingdom comprises four countries: England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland. Great Britain is just the island containing England, Scotland, and Wales. The Acts of Union of 1707 merged the English and Scottish parliaments. The British Empire was the largest empire in history, covering a quarter of the world's land area at its peak. Notable figures: William Shakespeare, Isaac Newton, Charles Darwin, Charles Dickens, the Beatles, Winston Churchill, Queen Elizabeth II (longest-reigning British monarch, 1952-2022), Alan Turing (father of computer science), Stephen Hawking, Adele, David Bowie, J.K. Rowling. Major industries: financial services (London is one of the world's two leading financial centers), pharmaceuticals (AstraZeneca, GSK), aerospace (Rolls-Royce), creative industries, energy. The UK left the European Union on January 31, 2020 (Brexit). London's Underground (the Tube) was the world's first underground railway (1863). The UK invented football (soccer), rugby, cricket, tennis, and golf. English common law is the basis of legal systems in dozens of countries. The UK has the world's fifth-largest economy. The Greenwich Meridian (0 degrees longitude) passes through London.

questions.question: What is the smallest country in both Europe and the world, located entirely within Rome?
questions.explanation: Capital: Vatican City (city-state). Population ~800. Official languages: Italian (for daily use) and Latin (for official church documents). The seat of the Roman Catholic Church, the world's largest Christian denomination with over 1.3 billion members. Ecclesiastical elective monarchy -- the Pope is both head of state and head of the Catholic Church, elected for life by the College of Cardinals. Vatican City was established as an independent state by the Lateran Treaty of 1929 between the Holy See and Italy under Mussolini. Area: only 121 acres (0.17 square miles), about one-eighth the size of Central Park. It is the smallest internationally recognized independent state in the world by both area and population. The current Pope is Francis (elected 2013), the first Jesuit pope, the first from the Americas, and the first from the Southern Hemisphere. Notable landmarks: St. Peter's Basilica (the largest church in the world), the Sistine Chapel (ceiling painted by Michelangelo, 1508-1512), the Vatican Museums (housing one of the world's greatest art collections including works by Raphael, Caravaggio, and Leonardo da Vinci). The Vatican has its own post office, radio station (Vatican Radio, established by Guglielmo Marconi in 1931), railway station, helipad, and pharmacy. The Vatican Bank (officially the Institute for the Works of Religion) manages church finances. Swiss Guards, who have protected the Pope since 1506, are the world's smallest army. The Vatican has its own top-level internet domain (.va) and issues its own euro coins, which are highly collectible. The crime rate per capita is technically among the highest in the world due to the tiny population and high tourist pickpocketing.
